column1: 27,"2020-10-19 00:00:00","62","2021-10-25 14:30:15","Let's Look Inside the Earth","Investigation","Nature","Students will analyze USGS seismology data in the classroom using spreadsheets and scatter plots to look for patterns and structure in the Earth’s crust.","
column4: src=""/images/activities/earthquake-map.jpg"" alt="" Image courtesy of geophysics.ou.edu"" width=""435"" height=""282"" />This project will be completed in groups of 2 or 3.

column1: Step 1: Select a location near active fault zones.
Choose a north-south or east-west slice of earth with high seismicity. See reference map at right.
Now that students have a general idea where earthquakes occur around the earth. Have teams of 2 select a north-south or east-west slice of earth with high seismicity. See reference map.
<li aria-level=""1"">Set date and time spans (choose at least 5 years).&nbsp
• Select geographic location. Use a vertical or horizontal slice.
• Set Format to CSV (comma separated values).
• Click Search and Download the file. (Important: You may need to rename the file to “my-earthquakes.csv”).
Step 3: Build the data in a spreadsheet.
• Open the downloaded CSV file in Excel or another spreadsheet tool.

• In order for your graph to display the depth data properly, the depth value must be negative, or the plot will be upside down. Make a new depth column. Right-click on the E column to the right of the depth column. Select Insert column to the left. The new column will now be E. In the first row of that column enter the label “Depth.” In row 2 of the new depth column, enter this equation: =D2*-1 and press Enter.
• Click on the new cell. Double click on the black node to the bottom left of the highlighted cell, or drag the square node down to the bottom of the dataset, so the equation fills the entire column.
Step 4: Graph the data as a scatter plot.

• Select the columns of data you wish to graph. One column will be the new Depth column. If E is that column, click E. Then hold CTRL and select the B or C column. If you want to graph an east-west slice, then you'll choose the longitude column. If you want to graph a north-south slice, then you'll choose the latitude column.
• Visualize the data. In Excel, click Chart or Scatter on the Insert menu and customize as needed. The horizontal x-axis should be the latitude or longitude data.
• The vertical y-axis will be the negative depth data. Zero depth will be the surface (if your location is at sea level).

column3: Sketch or another tool. Be sure to include East/West or North/South.

28,"2020-10-18 07:00:00","62","2021-01-04 04:10:45","Create Simple Topological Puzzles (Mazes)","Mini-lesson","Puzzles and Games","Students will learn how to collaborate with mathematics .","

Show students how to create a maze with one line.
• Draw a complex non-intersecting closed Jordan curve (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jordan_curve_theorem).
• Explain how Jordan Curves can be tested with an intersecting line (http://www-cgrl.cs.mcgill.ca/~godfried/teaching/cg-projects/97/Octavian/compgeom.html).
• Place a start (S) outside the curve and a finish (F) inside the curve.
• Erase one opening for the door to the maze.

Have them modify the method to create their own mazes. Show them how to create one, then share with a neighbor. By doing this, they will learn to collaborate with mathematics.

41,"2020-11-01 00:00:00","62","2022-08-23 00:22:28","Ancient Math Systems ","Investigation","Historical","Students will learn how to calculate using Egyptian, Roman, or Mayan mathematics and switching bases. Students will develop their own number system and justify/provide rationale for its use.","

For this investigation each student will submit a personalized PowerPoint project which contains:
• Name of civilization, location, and time period.
• What types of structures did they build?
• Did the study of astronomy affect their number system? If so, how.
• Does the number system have a base? If so, what is it and how did the base come to be? (Common bases were 5, 10, 20, and 60.)
• Did they use zero? If so, for what purpose?
• Show how to convert 179 to the number system.
• Explain how to add 12 + 28 in the number system.
• Explain how to multiply and divide in the number system.
• If fractions were used, how and why did they use fractions?
• Invent a number system for an alien civilization. How many fingers does the alien creature have? How often does its planet rotate around the star? Why does the alien civilization use numbers?
Examples of Civilizations and their Number Systems

55,"2020-11-16 00:00:00","62","2021-01-08 00:25:29","Fibonacci Art","Investigation","Art","Students can recognize recursive sequences and creatively apply Fibonacci squares or the Golden Mean in an art project.","

For this investigation each student will create one art project (line, color, mixed media, 3D, or computer-generated).
Students will make a list of recursive values they use in their artwork. They must include at least 5 iterations.
Students will use these values to construct shapes, objects, or design elements using a centimeter ruler or grid paper.
Students will submit their artwork as a photograph, scan or digital file.
Students will write at least one paragraph (minimum 3 sentences) explaining how the Fibonacci Sequence or the Golden Mean applies to their artwork.
Students will include their list of recursive values.
Clarity (Is the artwork neat, organized, and interesting to look at?)
Concepts (Has the student explained how the Fibonacci sequence or the Golden Mean applies to their artwork?)
Accuracy (Are there recursive sequence values correct?)
One way to set up your project is to make a list of recursive values based on the Fibonacci sequence, 1 in, 2 in, 3 in, 5 in, 8 in, 13 in. Then construct your artwork based on those values.
Similarly you could draw Fibonacci squares on paper, color them, cut them out, and rearrange them on paper in an interesting design.
You may also use the Golden Mean (Ratio) to determine your sizes. Remember the ratio between each successive size will be phi (Φ, the Golden Mean, which is 1.618...).
Select the smallest size of your object’s dimension, such as 2 cm.
Next multiple each dimension recursively by Φ (round to 1.6).
For 2 cm, the results would be:
Rocks of the proper dimensions (width) are stacked to produce an interesting design (top).
Fibonacci squares and spirals are colored and organized in an interesting design (middle).

For this investigation each student will illustrate a diagram showing how the Moon and Earth rotate and how they orbit around the Sun.
• View this web page and watch the video. https://clarkscience8.weebly.com/sun-earth-moon-motion.html
<li aria-level=""1"">Explore the Sun, Earth and Moon animation.&nbsp

<li aria-level=""1"">Configure the model so it will work in a diagram. Keep in mind that diagrams of the solar system are usually not to scale. If they were, you would not be able to practically fit them in a diagram. Since the Sun has a diameter 100 times larger than the Sun, you will need to scale the Earth so it will help users make sense of the rotation and orbital relationships. The Moon has a diameter of 1/4 the Earth, so that scale may be shown. Additionally, the Earth is 150 million kilometres (93 million miles) from the Sun. And the Moon is&nbsp
column2: 400,000 km (a quarter of a million miles) from Earth, so showing those relationships will be impossible.

• The Moon's rotational direction. Use curved arrows.
• The Moon's orbital path and direction around the Earth. Use ellipses and arrows.
• The Earth's orbital path and direction around the Sun. Use ellipses and arrows.
• Use pen, ink, color, marker, etc. Give the illustration some style. Make it clear.
• Final product will be a JPG file (graphic).

column1: 97,"2021-03-15 00:00:00","62","2021-03-15 17:54:39","Calculating Pi ","Investigation","Engineering","Students will use a physical model to determine the value of pi algebraically.","
column5: >Please collaborate with a partner (2 students only). If you would like to work alone, please contact your teacher.

column1: This math investigation is all about precision and accuracy. Calculate the value of the number pi (π) by measuring the circumference and diameter of a circle. Solve algebraically.
• Estimate the value for π.
<li>Make a plan. How will you find or draw a precise circle? How will you measure it properly? Plan must be writtens. Include steps.&nbsp
column2: Bulleted list is OK. Be sure to include details. Sketches are not required, but advised.

column1: • Construct or measure your circle. Measure both the circumference and the radius.
• Solve for π out to four decimal places. Older students can estimate their expected error. Please do not alter your values to adjust your estimation for π.
• Briefly research why π is important to an aspect of engineering or architecture. Please write two to three paragraphs and cite your sources.
• Submission: Word document containing the following (you are not required to create a PowerPoint).
• Your plan (if possible include a photo of you and/or your partner making measurements)
• Your value for π, please include your measurements
• Your research about using π in architecture or engineering
• Use string and chalk outside on a flat surface.
• Use string and dry erase marker on a window.
• Work small with a compass, protractor, magnifying glass, and a very sharp pencil!

column1: 99,"2021-03-22 00:00:00","62","2022-08-04 03:04:02","Bridge Design","Investigation","Engineering","Students will learn about three major bridge types while working with ratios and percentages and learning about three common functions.","<div style=""float: right
Please collaborate with a partner (3 students only). Each student in the group will need to research one bridge type.
This math investigation focuses on precision and accuracy.
• Challenge: How far can you extend uniform objects over the lip of a table?
• Locate a set of uniform (same size and dimensions) flat objects that can be stacked (dominoes, craft or popsicle sticks, books, Jenga blocks, etc.).
• Measure the long side of the object in centimeters.
• Extend one object over the side of the table. Measure the distance you able to extend it off the table in centimeters? What percentage of the whole length was over the air?

• Attempt to suspend as many of the objects (stacked) as far as you can over the lip of the table. Measure the distance you able to extend it off the table in centimeters? What percentage of the top object was beyond the lip of the table?
• Take a photo inline with the table edge.
• Briefly research one of the three main bridge types: beam, arch, suspension. Write two paragraphs in your own words describing that bridge type. Include at least three resources.
• Submission: Word document containing the following.

• Measurements and percentages
    Object length: __________cm
    One object: Distance over the air: __________cm
    One object: Percentage over the air: __________%
    Multiple objects: Distance over the air: __________cm
    Multiple objects: Percentage of top object over the air: __________%
    (We will use the value above to compare among groups.)

• One paragraph explaining why you think your objects extended further when stacked.
    Does it matter how the objects are stacked and spaced? Which worked best for you: linear, logarithmic, or exponential?
    Does mass of the object matter?
    Is there an optimal number of pieces?
• Your research (each student should research a different bridge type.)

122,"2021-01-18 00:00:00","62","2021-01-19 17:00:02","Algebra Transformers","Mini-lesson","Equality","Students will learn to fluidly transform and manipulate algebraic equations.","

Explain that when we solve algebraic equations we are essentially transforming both sides of equations until the variable is isolated. Provide a Rubrix cube as an example. Explain that the purpose of the equal sign is to keep our manipulations true. Before students are put into groups demonstrate how to transform an equation. Start with m=4. Add 3 to both sides. Scale the equation by multiplying by 5. Scale the equation again by multiplying by 1/2 (reinforce that this is dividing).
• Have each group secretly apply four transformations to the equations: add, subtract, multiply or divide (advanced groups can use powers and roots).
• Groups return with their new equations. If remote, paste into chat.
• Groups solve and return new equation, which should still equal to m=3
Repeat process with three transformation. Explain, like the phone game, precision is essential when solving algebraic equations.

This project is for individuals.
• Build a 3x3x3 cube of 1 centimeter or 1 inch wooden cubes.
• Number of cubes per puzzle piece must be no less than 4 and no greater than 6.
• Combined pieces should not construct a flat plane.
• Build your puzzle. Use only one small drop of wood glue per connection. Do not over-glue or your puzzle with not disassemble.
• Wipe off any extra glue. Let dry for a few minutes.
<li>Use painters tape to clamp your puzzle together as a cube.&nbsp
• Disassemble carefully. Use a utility knife if necessary. Re-glue any pieces that fall off in the process.
• Use a stopwatch to time six classmates for solving your puzzle. Create a histogram of the timings.
• What steps could you take to determine all the different ways to construct a 4- to 6-cube piece?
• What does the histogram tell you about your puzzle?

136,"2021-03-01 00:00:00","62","2021-02-26 18:13:26","Solving Quadratic Equations Using the Area Model","Mini-lesson","Functions","Students will solve quadratic equations using the area model and plot solutions and equation on a graphing calculator.","

Step 1: Remind students how the area model works with multiplication.
Step 2: Mix-up. Show students how to create quadratic equations with two factors using the area model. Graph beginning and ending equations to demonstrate equality.
Step 3: Unmix: Show students how to factor the equation created using area model (working backwards).
Step 4: Groups of two will work together to create a quadratic equation. 'Mix it up' into the standard format. Share with main group.
Step 5. Another group will solve the quadratic equation using the area model and graph both the factors and the final equation to check their work.
Explain other ways to solve quadratic equations, such as the quadratic formula

column1: 255,"2023-04-08 00:00:00","62","2023-05-02 17:13:45","Animal Vision","Investigation","Nature","Student will construct and measure binocular and monocular vision angles for one species of animal, while constructing a model for apparent vision.","<table style=""border: 0px
For groups of two (individuals OK). Compile documentation in Word or PowerPoint.
• Select an animal (insect, mammal, bird, fish, etc.).
• Find a top-view of the animal's head or skull.
• Determine the monocular, binocular, and blind area of animal's vision. Draw a diagram with each vision area labeled.

• Research the color vision ability of the animal. If it has more than two eyes, non-circular pupils, or compound lenses, determine how those features affect vision. Construct a photographic model using GIMP or another photo manipulation tool.

column2: Peer Review before you complete your diagram and vision model.

column1: • Answer the following questions (minimum three paragraphs):
• Why does the animal have a wide peripheral or binocular vision?
• What advantage does color or black and white vision provide the species?
• If the species has compound lenses or non-circular pupils, how does that affect vision?
• Top-view diagram with angles (monocular, binocular, and blind).
• Understanding the retinal basis of vision across species
• Why do animal pupils come in different shapes?

column1: 153,"2021-03-29 00:00:00","62","2021-03-29 17:47:38","Three Sheet Tower","Investigation","Engineering","Students will learn about the engineering design process while building a tower out of paper.","<div style=""float: right
Please collaborate with a partner (3 students only).
This math investigation focuses on the Engineering Design Process Cycle.

column2: A high tower must be built to support at least five pennies. The winning group will have the highest value for their ""tower evaluation index."" Tower evaluation index is calculated by multiplying the height (cm) by the number of pennies.

column1: • Identify Criteria and Constraints: Only three sheets of copy paper may be used. Colored copy paper or notebook paper is OK. You may cut and fold your paper, but no tape or glue may be used.

column2: Add additional versions of your main idea.

column1: • Select an Approach: Why did you select your approach?
• Build a Model or Prototype: Please include a photo.
• Refine the Design. What modifications did you need to make to your final solution?
Please submit a Word document (PowerPoint is OK) which contains:

<li>Information about how you applied all eight steps listed above to your project. Be specific. Please use full sentences, but keep the length of each step to less than one paragraph. The first two steps have been done for you.&nbsp
• Measurement of the area of your base in square centimeters
• Number of pennies stacked on the top
• Tower evaluation index: height x number of pennies
• Build with strong shapes. Triangles are strong and can be inserted into squares.
• Rotate your shapes, so the paper holds in corners by compression. Otherwise, tension will pull your shapes apart.

• Select a concept to teach: Please choose a Common Core State Standard. Fill in your name in the final column of the standards spreadsheet. If a standard has already been selected, please choose another (one per student). A good choice will be a concept which will include a graphical representation. Geometric concepts will work well, but many principles and theorems can be selected (examples). Please choose one with which you are familiar at your grade level or higher. • Peer Review: Provide your Inforgraphic to at least two students. Each students will provide two brief comments for your infographic

202,"2021-09-15 00:00:00","62","2021-09-21 18:18:07","International Dot Day","Investigation","Art","Students will adjust circle equation values and points on a coordinate plane to create circle and dot art of their favorite fruit.","

This is an Individual project.
• Use Desmos (or Geogebra) to create circle equations or points on a coordinate plane to build still life art of various fruit.
• Circle equation example: (x-3)^2 + (y-(+1))^2 = 5^2. This equation places a circle of radius of 5, centered at (3,-1).
• With Desmos you can adjust the color and alpha (transparency) of your circle by using the gear icon.
•  Use colored construction paper, glue sticks, a compass, and scissors to cut and arrange perfect circles into your fruit still life artwork.
• Use as few dots as possible to create your fruit, but enough to make an interesting and balanced work of art.
• Only cut a dot in half if necessary.
• Final project as digital file from scan, photo, or screen capture.
• Custom Colors Google Chrome Extension for Desmos

column2: >Prompt 1: Hi, I'm Lisandra, business manager of Indigenous Northwest. We help Northwest tribal communities market eco-indigenous tours. We're looking for someone that can make a good logo for our business. I would like a 3-color design logo. Can you help us promote our company?

216,"2021-12-28 00:00:00","62","2022-01-03 21:02:02","Get Set Jet Set","Investigation","Combinations and Permutations","Students will use discrete mathematics to determine the best possible route between four major world-wide cities.","

An international airline company has awarded you 25,000 air miles for being an awesome math student. Three of your friends may travel with you (group project). You want to visit three of the major continents (South America, Africa, and Euro-Asia). You have been tasked to find the most direct, efficient, and feasible route.
• On which type(s) of aircraft will you travel to get to each destination?
• You may only fly in and out of one of the listed city (with exception of your home city).
• You may fly to as many additional cities as you'd like, but they must be in the set of the 30 largest.
• The airplane you select for each route cannot refuel at an unlisted airport.
• Crossing the Pacific Ocean is OK, if you select an airplane that can make the long journey without refueling.
• Choose an efficient airplane (for example: don't choose a jumbo jet for a short leg of your journey)
• Write a justification explaining why your route is the most direct, efficient, and feasible (minimum three paragraphs).
• Include a photo from each destination (group selfies are OK).
• Map your destinations with route lines. Modify this map.
• How many possible routes are between any two of the 30 largest cities?
• Why are the lines straight on the Desmos Graph but curved on the Distance Calculator?
• Make a T-chart of project tasks with who did which task.
• List of 30 largest cities (IDs correlate with Desmos Map): https://docs.google.com/spreadsheets/d/1Ycv_XBvQ24C5O0kaCHP9mTLdrYVOCIY3KZoNplhnFb0/edit?usp=sharing

column1: 220,"2022-01-26 00:00:00","62","2022-01-26 20:28:26","Tic-Tac-Toe Products","Mini-lesson","Numbers","Students will develop strategies for creating products from numbers.","<ol>
• Player Orange and Player Purple each select one factor, 1 – 13 at the bottom of the page and place one of the gray markers on that factor.
• Player Orange may move only one of the two markers to a new factor. Player Orange then places a marker on the grid covering the product of the two factors.
• Player Purple may move only one marker to make a new product and place their marker on the grid. The markers can both be placed on the same factor. For example 5 x 3 = 15.

• Players alternate moving one factor marker at a time and continue placing their markers on the grid until a player has marked four products in a row. After the game players should discuss their strategies.

From Helping with Math at Home: More Ideas for Parents. 2006. Portsmouth, NH: Heinemann.

226,"2022-03-18 00:00:00","62","2022-07-05 14:21:33","Slo-Mo Glaciers","Investigation","Modeling","Students will calculate physical characteristics and flow rate of one glacier and determine its hydrological downstream impacts.","

This project is for groups of two. Please create a PowerPoint or Word Document.
• Select one glacier from the Pacific Northwest.
• Measure the surface area of the glacier using Google Earth.
<li>Estimate mass balance (Is it growing or shrinking? Do researchers have a rate on its mass change?)<ol style=""list-style-type: lower-alpha
• Use historical imagery. Be sure to compare glacier images during months when it doesn't have any new snow on it (August, September, and October),
• Or review scientific research about your glacier.
<li>Determine the rate of movement on one section of the glacier.<ol style=""list-style-type: lower-alpha
• Find a rock or surface feature on the glacier using Google Earth.
• Calculate rate in meters per year (movement will probably be less than 1000 meters per year).
• Research: Which downstream communities rely on water from the glacier?
• What type of economic activities rely on the water from the glacier?
• Glacier name and location (decimal-based latitude and longitude)
• List of communities who rely on the water from your glacier

column2: rely on the water from your glacier

column1: • Answer the following question: How do glaciers affect river flow during the summer dry months in the Pacific Northwest?
Vocab: cirque, glacial mass balance, glacial melt-off, glacier, hydrology, moraine, precipitation, water year

Researchers in the Pacific Northwest are monitoring glacial mass to learn how yearly glacial melting affects rivers in the Northwest. They are finding that glaciers act as a kind of buffer to drought. During times when precipitation is low and temperatures are high, glaciers release water into rivers. The effect is that many rivers in the Northwest do not fluctuate as much as they would without glaciers.
• How to measure flow rate and volume of a glacier
• Finding average depth and area of a glacier using Riemann sum and calculus

column4: >Safety Tips for Working with a Drill Press

column5: >Always wear safety goggles or a face shield.
>No loose clothing, gloves or jewelry should be worn when working on the drill press.
>Be sure the drill bit is locked securely in the chuck. Remember that all adjustments should be made with the power off. The chuck key must be removed from the chuck before turning on the power.
>Always disconnect the drill from the power source when making repairs.

>Hold the workpiece firmly so that it will not fly or spin off the table. It is generally best to fasten the workpiece securely with clamps or hold it in a vise. This is especially true when drilling or boring small workpieces.
>Keep hands and fingers at least 3” from rotating drill bits.&nbsp
>Adjust the table so that the hole in the table center is beneath the drill, or set the depth stop to avoid drilling into the table.
>Use a brush to keep the table and workpiece free of sawdust or chips. Always disconnect the machine from the power source before cleaning.

>Never try to stop the machine by grabbing the chuck after the power is turned off. Do not run the tool unattended. Turn off the power, and do not leave the drill press until the chuck comes to a complete stop.

column1: 243,"2022-10-04 00:00:00","62","2023-05-19 14:00:58","Chlorophyll Chromatography","Investigation","Ratios","Students will analyze the Rf values for two species of plant material and analyze those values in a classroom spreadsheet.","
column3: src=""/images/activities/Chromatography_of_chlorophyll_results.jpg"" alt=""Chromatography of chlorophyll results"" width=""204"" height=""373"" />This is a group project (two or three). Submit as a Code.org Web Lab project.

column1: • Strips of chromatography paper (or coffee filter, fitting in test tubes)
• Finely cut up some leaves and fill a mortar to about 2 cm depth.Add a pinch of sand and about six drops of isopropyl alcohol (70%) from the pipette.
• Grind the mixture with a pestle for at least three minutes.
• On a strip of chromatography paper (or coffee filter strips), draw a pencil line 2 cm from the bottom.
• Use a toothpick to put liquid from the leaf extract onto the centre of the line. Keep the spot as small as possible.

• Allow the spot to dry, then add another spot on top. Add five more drops of solution, letting each one dry before putting on the next. The idea is to build up a very concentrated small spot on the paper.
• Attach the paper to a toothpick with tape so that when placed in the test tube, the paper is just clear of its base. Test fit it in test tube.
• Place 2 mL of isopropyl alcohol in the bottom of the test tube. Ensure the level is below where the spot is on the paper, but so the bottom of the paper will soak in the isopropyl alcohol.
<li>Allow the paper to sit in tube and dry over night.&nbsp

• Record your data. Measure each color band from the original spot up to the center of the color band. Create Rf value (ratio), by dividing that measurement by the maximum distance the solvent traveled up the paper.
• Record your data in the class database.
• After all data is recorded make data observations. Write a minimum of one paragraph explaining what you observe.
• What species did you test (Latin name, habitat)?

column8: src=""/images/activities/Wind-map-with-temps.jpg"" alt=""Wind map with temps"" width=""325"" height=""232"" />You and two friends will be flying a Cessna 172 from Renton to Spokane, Washington. Your grandma will be picking you up at 5 pm. She expects you to be on time and so does the control tower operator! What is your ETD (estimated time of departure)?

column2: >Keep in mind cruising speed for your small aircraft is 120 knots. Renton airport expects a flight plan which includes a minimum of four waypoints for your flight. Even though it's a direct flight, waypoints should be over cities or small towns (though you won't be stopping). Keep in mind your fuel tank will limit you to 250 nautical miles. No crash landings!

>You will run into some wind. Take a look at the forecast wind map for the day of your flight (looks like a cold arctic blast is coming too!). How will you adjust your headings because of the wind you will encounter? For each of the four waypoint in between Renton and Spokane (remember to add Renton and Spokane to your map), you will need to determine your heading (degrees from true north). Use this tool to help you visual wind vectors in order to determine your actual forward velocity and heading.
>While you're on your flight, please keep a flight log.&nbsp

column1: 268,"2024-01-19 00:00:00","62","2024-02-05 19:15:45","Thrifty Kalimbas","Investigation","Music","Student will learn how to build and tune a kalimba (thumb piano) keys to the pentatonic or another major scale.","
column3: src=""/images/activities/thrift-store-boxes.JPG"" alt=""thrift store boxes"" width=""400"" height=""281"" />Individual project. Compile documentation in Word document.&nbsp

column1: • Visit a local thrift store and select an inexpensive wooden box for a resonating chamber.
• 2 metal coat hanger (classic, full-length)
• 2 small hex carriage bolts with wingnuts smaller than 1/4 inch in diameter.
• 1/4 x 3/8 x 4 inch piece of hardwood to clamp the keys

• Build Key Base. Snip 1 length of coat hanger wire, 4 inches long (or a size that will fit within the edges of your box). This will be the base on which your keys will be clamped. Using pliers, bend the ends of this wire into right angles about 1/8-inch from both ends. Position the wire in the desired location on the box. Drill pilot holes. Hammer the crimped ends of the wire perpendicular to where the keys will lay.

• Attach Key Clamp. Drill holes the size of the carriage bolts through the hardwood clamp and the box next to the key base, 1/3 the distance from either end of the key base. Insert the carriage bolts through box and key clamp. Attach loosely with the wingnuts.
• Construct notes. Using coat hanger wire, snip 7 or 8 lengths of wire ranging from 3 to 5 inches. These are your keys. Hammer out flat 1/2 inch of one end of each wire.
• Attach notes. Insert each note alternatively right to left, with the lowest note in your scale at the center of your kalimba. Loosely tighten the wingnuts, so the notes can be tuned.
<li><span>Tune your Kalimba. Decide on your scale, based on the lengths of your keys. Use a tuner or the&nbsp
column2: Flute Tuner. Begin with the longest (center) key. This should be your fundamental note in your key. For example, in C-major scale, your fundamental note will be C, then alternating, you will tune the keys to D, E, F, G, A, B, and C. If you are using less than 8 keys, tune your kalimba to a major pentatonic scale.

column4: >Safety Tips for Working with a Drill Press

column5: >Always wear safety goggles or a face shield.
>No loose clothing, gloves or jewelry should be worn when working on the drill press.
>Be sure the drill bit is locked securely in the chuck. Remember that all adjustments should be made with the power off. The chuck key must be removed from the chuck before turning on the power.
>Always disconnect the drill from the power source when making repairs.

>Hold the workpiece firmly so that it will not fly or spin off the table. It is generally best to fasten the workpiece securely with clamps or hold it in a vise. This is especially true when drilling or boring small workpieces.
>Keep hands and fingers at least 3” from rotating drill bits.&nbsp
>Adjust the table so that the hole in the table center is beneath the drill, or set the depth stop to avoid drilling into the table.
>Use a brush to keep the table and workpiece free of sawdust or chips. Always disconnect the machine from the power source before cleaning.

>Never try to stop the machine by grabbing the chuck after the power is turned off. Do not run the tool unattended. Turn off the power, and do not leave the drill press until the chuck comes to a complete stop.

This project will be completed individually.
Step 1: Watch these videos and read the article. Take some notes.
• The Vibrance of Natural Colorr
Step 2: Select and sketch a colorful lifeform (plant, animal, fungi, etc.)
• Lifeform must have at least three bright colors.

• Use colored pencils or digital ink to sketch three highly-simplified design options. Focus on basic geometric shapes.
    Advanced students will need to refine their design, considering that theirs will be in 3D.
    Middle school students may use another tool which requires the use of fundamental shapes such as PowerPoint.
• Determine the RGB color model for each color.
• Design your graphic using Desmos Calculator. Advanced students use Desmos 3D. Use a white background.
<li aria-level=""1"">Define your colors in 3x8-bit (0-255) RGB color and hexadecimal code. Demonstrate mathematically the conversion between RGB and hexadecimal for one of the colors.&nbsp
• Save your Desmos design. Screenshot/snip your graphic as large as possible on white background without grid, axes, and numbers.
• For each color theory video, list one interesting thing you learned.
• What is the ecological purpose of the colors in your lifeform?
• What are the underlying physics which form your colors?
• List three colors with 8-bit RGB color and hexadecimal definitions
